([Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_language "Georgian language"): საქართველოს ეროვნული საფეხბურთო ნაკრები, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Georgian "Romanization of Georgian"): sakartvelos erovnuli sapekhburto nak'rebi) represents [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches, and is controlled by the [Georgian Football Federation](/wiki/Georgian_Football_Federation "Georgian Football Federation"), the governing body for [football in Georgia](/wiki/Football_in_Georgia_(country) "Football in Georgia (country)"). It is a member of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") in Europe and [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in global competitions.

The **Georgia national football team** ([Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_language "Georgian language"): საქართველოს ეროვნული საფეხბურთო ნაკრები, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Georgian "Romanization of Georgian"): sakartvelos erovnuli sapekhburto nak'rebi) represents [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches, and is controlled by the [Georgian Football Federation](/wiki/Georgian_Football_Federation "Georgian Football Federation"), the governing body for [football in Georgia](/wiki/Football_in_Georgia_(country) "Football in Georgia (country)"). It is a member of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") in Europe and [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in global competitions.

The Georgian team's first match took place on 27 May 1990 against [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_national_football_team "Lithuania national football team"), while Georgia was still part of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). The team have attempted to qualify for each major tournament from [Euro 1996](/wiki/1996_UEFA_European_Football_Championship "1996 UEFA European Football Championship") onwards. Their efforts culminated in their first successful qualification for a major tournament, the [UEFA Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024"), secured with a win against [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team") in the [UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying play-off final](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_qualifying_play-offs "UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying play-offs").

The team is colloquially referred to as Jvarosnebi ([The Crusaders](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades")) and has adopted [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George") as their symbol, which is a [patron](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of Georgia.

Upon its admission into FIFA ranked 156th, Georgia ascended to their peak [World Ranking](/wiki/FIFA_Men%27s_World_Ranking "FIFA Men's World Ranking") of 42nd in 1998. The team plays their home games at the [Boris Paichadze Dinamo Arena](/wiki/Boris_Paichadze_Dinamo_Arena "Boris Paichadze Dinamo Arena") in [Tbilisi](/wiki/Tbilisi "Tbilisi").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Football was introduced to Georgia by [English](/wiki/English_people "English people") sailors in early 20th century. [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") ships that docked at the harbors of [Poti](/wiki/Poti "Poti") and [Batumi](/wiki/Batumi "Batumi") frequently hosted football matches among their crew, attracting the attention of [Lelo burti](/wiki/Lelo_burti "Lelo burti") players, who quickly embraced and adapted the sport. While the exact date of the first football match in Georgia remains uncertain, football was first mentioned in the Georgian press in 1890.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From 1923 to 1990, Georgian football players were part of the [USSR national team](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_football_team "Soviet Union national football team"), with two of them, [Murtaz Khurtsilava](/wiki/Murtaz_Khurtsilava "Murtaz Khurtsilava") and [Aleksandre Chivadze](/wiki/Aleksandre_Chivadze "Aleksandre Chivadze"), serving as captains at different times.[[4]](#cite_note-4) During the early Soviet era, Georgia competed in the Trans-Caucasian Championship, hosting the 1926 edition. Georgia's first game came against [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan_national_football_team "Azerbaijan national football team") in Tbilisi, losing 4–2 in [Tbilisi](/wiki/Tbilisi "Tbilisi"). The Georgian SSR's first win came in their second game, beating [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia_national_football_team "Armenia national football team") 7–0. Georgia would later host the 1927 and 1928 editions of the Trans-Caucasian Championship, winning the latter edition after a 5–1 and 6–0 win over Armenia and Azerbaijan respectively. After a six year hiatus, Georgia played in the 1934 competition in Azerbaijan, before once against hosting the 1935 Trans-Caucasian Championship, winning it once again.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

During this period, the USSR national team included players from various [Soviet republics](/wiki/Republics_of_the_Soviet_Union "Republics of the Soviet Union"), including [Georgian SSR](/wiki/Georgian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Georgian Soviet Socialist Republic"). Georgian players made significant contributions to the team, with several becoming key figures and achieving notable success, such as [Slava Metreveli](/wiki/Slava_Metreveli "Slava Metreveli") scoring in the [1960 European Nations' Cup final](/wiki/1960_European_Nations%27_Cup_final "1960 European Nations' Cup final").[[7]](#cite_note-7) One of the most famous Georgian footballers who played for the USSR national team was Murtaz Khurtsilava, who captained the team and played a crucial role in the [1966 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"), where the USSR finished in fourth place. Another prominent player was [Vitaly Daraselia](/wiki/Vitaly_Daraselia "Vitaly Daraselia"), known for his performances in the 1980s, along with [David Kipiani](/wiki/David_Kipiani "David Kipiani"), [Ramaz Shengelia](/wiki/Ramaz_Shengelia "Ramaz Shengelia") and [Vladimir Gutsaev](/wiki/Vladimir_Gutsaev "Vladimir Gutsaev").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Following Soviet rule in Georgia, the contemporary history of the Georgia national football team began in 1990, when the team played their first international match against [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_national_football_team "Lithuania national football team"), the first country to accept an invitation. The match was held on 27 May 1990 at the [national stadium](/wiki/Boris_Paichadze_Dinamo_Arena "Boris Paichadze Dinamo Arena").[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) Georgia were coached by [Givi Nodia](/wiki/Givi_Nodia "Givi Nodia"). The friendly match ended in a 2–2 draw. This was the only match prior to the declaration of [independence](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Georgia) "Independence Day (Georgia)") on 9 April 1991. Soon afterwards the team played another friendly match against [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova_national_football_team "Moldova national football team").

The [Georgian Football Federation](/wiki/Georgian_Football_Federation "Georgian Football Federation") became a member of both [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") and [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in 1992,[[10]](#cite_note-10) enabling Georgia to play competitive matches. The first of these came in September 1994, a 1–0 defeat to [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova_national_football_team "Moldova national football team")[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) as part of the [qualifiers](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996_qualifying_Group_7 "UEFA Euro 1996 qualifying Group 7") for [Euro 1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996"). Georgia finished third in their group, ahead of Moldova, [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") and [Albania](/wiki/Albania_national_football_team "Albania national football team"), but seven points behind second-placed qualifier [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_national_football_team "Bulgaria national football team").

Georgia failed to [qualify](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_2 "1998 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 2") for the [1998 World Cup](/wiki/1998_World_Cup "1998 World Cup") in France, obtaining 10 points and finishing in fourth place, level on points with [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team"). At this time Georgia reached 42nd place in the [FIFA World Ranking](/wiki/FIFA_World_Ranking "FIFA World Ranking").

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Georgia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 21st century")]

During [UEFA Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000") [qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000_qualifying_Group_2 "UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying Group 2") the Georgia national team won one match, drew two and finished at the bottom of the group with five points.

The team finished fifth (and last) with seven points in their qualifying [group](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004_qualifying_Group_10 "UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying Group 10") for [Euro 2004](/wiki/Euro_2004 "Euro 2004"), although they defeated [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_football_team "Russia national football team") with a goal scored by [Malkhaz Asatiani](/wiki/Malkhaz_Asatiani "Malkhaz Asatiani").

In the [2006 World Cup](/wiki/2006_World_Cup "2006 World Cup") [qualifiers](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_-_UEFA_Group_2 "2006 FIFA World Cup qualification - UEFA Group 2") Georgia beat [Albania](/wiki/Albania_national_football_team "Albania national football team") 2–0 at home and [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_national_football_team "Kazakhstan national football team") away 2–1. They finished sixth of seven with ten points in Group 2.

Georgia were sixth out of seven teams in the [Euro 2008](/wiki/Euro_2008 "Euro 2008") [qualifying group](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying_Group_B "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying Group B") with ten points. They defeated [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") 2–0 at home and the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands_national_football_team "Faroe Islands national football team") 6–0 away and 3–1 at home.

[Héctor Cúper](/wiki/H%C3%A9ctor_C%C3%BAper "Héctor Cúper") became the manager of Georgia in August 2008. During the [qualification](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_8 "2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 8") round for the [2010 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup") Georgia failed to win any matches and finished sixth (and last) with three points.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Cuper didn't extend his contract, and on 6 November 2009, [Temur Ketsbaia](/wiki/Temur_Ketsbaia "Temur Ketsbaia") was appointed as the new manager of the Georgian national team. Ketsbaia resigned as manager after a 4–0 defeat at home to Poland in the [Euro 2016](/wiki/Euro_2016 "Euro 2016") [qualifiers](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying_Group_D "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying Group D") on 14 November 2014, having previously stated he would do so before the match regardless of the result.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/TUR-GEO_18.06.24%2C_Batum_%284%29.jpg/250px-TUR-GEO_18.06.24%2C_Batum_%284%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:TUR-GEO_18.06.24,_Batum_(4).jpg)

Georgian fans in the Batumi fan zone celebrating their first goal vs [Turkey](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_Group_F#Turkey_vs_Georgia "UEFA Euro 2024 Group F") in [Euro 2024](/wiki/Euro_2024 "Euro 2024"), their first goal in the history of the tournament.

In June 2016, Georgia beat the two-time reigning European champions [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team") 1–0 in their final pre-Euro 2016 friendly.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 2018, they were the first team to earn promotion in the new [UEFA Nations League](/wiki/UEFA_Nations_League "UEFA Nations League"). They scored the event's first goal in a [UEFA Nations League D](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_UEFA_Nations_League_D "2018–19 UEFA Nations League D") game in [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_national_football_team "Kazakhstan national football team") before beating [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia_national_football_team "Latvia national football team") twice and [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra_national_football_team "Andorra national football team"), with two games still to spare and earned their first-ever playoff berth.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) In the [UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying"), Georgia had a disappointing run, with their only two wins came over [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar_national_football_team "Gibraltar national football team"). In the Path D playoff semi-final, Georgia managed to beat [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_national_football_team "Belarus national football team") 1–0 and thus the hope to qualify for [UEFA Euro 2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020") increased,[[17]](#cite_note-17) but it went in vain after the Georgians suffered a heartbreaking home defeat to [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia_national_football_team "North Macedonia national football team") in the decisive match and thus missed the opportunity to make a historic debut in a major competition.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Starting lineup in Georgia's 2–0 win against Portugal at [UEFA Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_Group_F "UEFA Euro 2024 Group F").

Georgia showed improvements with a strong 2–0 win over [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team") at the [2022 World Cup qualification](/wiki/2022_World_Cup_qualification "2022 World Cup qualification") on 11 November 2021.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) They continued their improvement by winning the [2022–23 UEFA Nations League C Group 4](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_UEFA_Nations_League_C "2022–23 UEFA Nations League C"), getting promoted to [2024-25 UEFA Nations League B](/wiki/2024-25_UEFA_Nations_League_B "2024-25 UEFA Nations League B"), and qualified for their second-ever [play-off berth](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_qualifying_play-offs "UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying play-offs").[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Georgia won 2–0 against [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_national_football_team "Luxembourg national football team"), earning them a spot in the final of the [UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying play-offs](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_qualifying_play-offs "UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying play-offs").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) They defeated [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team") 4–2 on penalties after a 0–0 draw in the playoff final, which guaranteed Georgia's qualification for their first ever international tournament at [Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

During Euro 2024, Georgia were placed in Group F; they subsequently lost 3–1 in the match against [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team"), where [Georges Mikautadze](/wiki/Georges_Mikautadze "Georges Mikautadze") scored the first-ever goal in Georgia's history in the Euros, and drew 1–1 against the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_national_football_team "Czech Republic national football team"). Their 2–0 victory against [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team") was considered one of the biggest upsets in European Championship history,[[34]](#cite_note-34) and was enough to propel Georgia to the round of 16 in their first international tournament appearance. Georgia subsequently played eventual [Champions](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_Final "UEFA Euro 2024 Final") [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team") in the Round of 16, and lost by a score of 4–1.[[35]](#cite_note-35) [Georges Mikautadze](/wiki/Georges_Mikautadze "Georges Mikautadze") finished the Euro 2024 campaign as the joint-[UEFA European Football Championship Top Goalscorer](/wiki/UEFA_European_Football_Championship "UEFA European Football Championship").
